data.title: Renewables industry reels as Senate megabill unveils new excise tax
data.description:
The renewable energy sector was caught off guard this weekend by a proposed Senate tax targeting wind and solar projects that use components from China, marking a sharp escalation in efforts to dismantle incentives for clean energy.

The measure, part of broader negotiations over President Trump’s major tax and spending package, aims to roll back several energy tax credits expanded under the Biden administration. With lawmakers still debating how to fund tax cuts and increased military and border spending, clean energy developers say each new version of the bill has created growing uncertainty for their industry, The Wall Street Journal reported Sunday

The latest provision would apply to wind and solar projects placed in service after 2027 if they rely on a certain percentage of Chinese-made components. That would be a significant blow given China’s dominance in supplying critical minerals, batteries and other materials.

Tesla (TSLA) Chief Executive Elon Musk, reacting on social media, called the proposal “utterly insane and destructive,” arguing that it favors legacy industries at the expense of emerging ones.

The Senate bill also tightens qualification rules for renewable energy tax credits, requiring projects to be operational by 2027 rather than simply started, a tougher hurdle for developers facing grid connection delays.

U.S. Chamber of Commerce executive Neil Bradley voiced mixed feelings, calling the package “strong and pro-growth” but warning that taxing energy production, whether fossil fuels or renewables, will raise electricity costs and should be avoided, the Journal reported.

The stakes are high as electricity demand rises sharply, driven by booming data center construction fueled by the AI industry. Renewable energy developers contend their projects are essential to meeting that demand, as they make up most of the near-term generation in the pipeline.

The Trump administration, meanwhile, is pushing for more constant power generation, such as natural gas, but supply chain constraints have made expanding that capacity difficult.

The Senate proposal also accelerates the phaseout of electric vehicle tax credits, ending them in September for most vehicles. That’s earlier than the House’s version, which sunsets them at year-end. That change would affect Tesla (TSLA) customers, who rely on federal incentives for EV purchases, solar installations, and battery storage systems.

Industry groups warn that these changes could increase energy costs, hand more market share to China, and result in U.S. job losses. But time is running out to sway Republican lawmakers, with Trump pushing for the bill to reach his desk by Friday.

Dear Readers: We recognize that politics often intersect with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Senate Republicans reinstate Medicaid cuts to salvage Trump’s budget
data.description:
Senate Republicans revived significant cuts to Medicaid as part of Donald Trump’s flagship economic package, modifying the proposal to clear procedural hurdles and satisfy demands from fiscal conservatives, the Associated Press reported Sunday.

The reductions to Medicaid, the government health program for low-income and disabled Americans, are intended to help offset the bill’s revenue losses from tax cuts, a key priority for Republican budget hawks.

While the adjustment strengthens the bill’s fiscal framework, it risks alienating pivotal moderates, including Senators Susan Collins of Maine, Lisa Murkowski of Alaska and Thom Tillis of North Carolina, who have been advocating for scaled-back cuts to Medicaid.

Senate GOP Leader John Thune is under pressure to balance the competing demands of conservative and moderate lawmakers as he pushes to deliver the sweeping tax and spending bill ahead of Trump’s July 4 deadline for passage.

Earlier, Senate parliamentarian Elizabeth MacDonough ruled that several healthcare provisions could not advance under the fast-track process Republicans are using to bypass Democratic opposition. That decision had temporarily removed around $250 billion in proposed Medicaid savings sought by fiscal conservatives.

However, Democrats on the Senate Budget Committee said Sunday that MacDonough approved a revised measure restricting states’ ability to levy taxes on healthcare providers to generate federal matching funds for Medicaid. It remains unclear how much this version would save the federal government.

The Congressional Budget Office has warned that the legislation, in its previous form, could result in millions losing health coverage, projecting that an earlier draft would leave 11.8 million people without health benefits by 2033.

The proposed changes could have financial implications for hospital groups such as HCA Healthcare (HCA) and Tenet Healthcare (THC), which often rely on state-level provider taxes to boost Medicaid reimbursements.

According to budget analysts, a more moderate version of this proposal in the House would have saved roughly $89 billion over a decade.

Meanwhile, other measures designed to increase Medicaid funding for Alaska and Hawaii, aimed at securing support from Murkowski and other lawmakers, were struck down by the parliamentarian for violating Senate rules. Murkowski ultimately voted to advance the legislation after negotiating for Alaska-specific benefits.

The parliamentarian also blocked provisions that would have delayed federal initiatives to streamline healthcare enrollment and suspend the Biden administration’s nursing home staffing requirements. Without those provisions, Republicans risk losing an estimated $190 billion in potential savings, or $167 billion from delaying enrollment reforms and $23 billion from pausing the staffing rule.

Trump’s legislative centerpiece seeks to permanently extend the individual and corporate tax cuts enacted in 2017 while introducing new temporary breaks for overtime pay, tipped workers, seniors and vehicle buyers.

The package also calls for significant new investments in the military, border security and immigration enforcement. To help finance the bill, Republicans propose cuts to Medicaid, food assistance programs and student financial aid.

data.title: Weekly gainers & losers in tech sector: Arista Networks leads, Palantir ends in red
data.description:
Wall Street’s major averages closed Friday's trading session higher, notching fresh all-time trading highs and record high closes, as investors reacted to a hotter-than-expected May core PCE report and welcomed news of a newly inked U.S.-China trade agreement.

Deutsche Bank's Jim Reid noted that the continued positive momentum in equities was impressive. “We seem to be in a sweet spot, post-Middle Eastern calm and pre-July 9th reciprocal tariff extension deadline” Reid added.

The US and China have finalized a trade truce reached last month, with Beijing agreeing to supply rare earths in exchange for the lifting of US countermeasures. The White House also has imminent plans to reach agreements with 10 major trading partners.

Much of the market’s recent strength has been driven by a select group of high-performing stocks that have delivered outsized year-to-date gains. These companies have not only outpaced the broader index but have also been instrumental in fueling its historic climb.

The Technology Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:XLK) rose more than 3% throughout the week, outperforming the broader benchmark index, which grew 2.46%. 

Let’s take a look at this week’s gainers and losers in the tech industry:

Gainers: 

Arista Networks (NYSE:ANET) +15.23%: Arista Networks moved higher after Evercore ISI said it expects the networking company to sustain 20%-plus revenue growth due in part to its partnerships with Meta Platforms (META) and major cloud providers.

Enphase Energy (NASDAQ:ENPH) +14.03%: Enphase Energy closed to top the S&P 500 leaderboard on Thursday, following reports that the U.S. Senate may not cut federal tax incentives for residential rooftop solar installations.

Advanced Micro Devices (NASDAQ:AMD) +12.14%: Melius Research upgraded AMD to Buy from Hold, citing its potential in inferencing.

NVIDIA (NASDAQ:NVDA) +9.66%: Loop Capital Markets raised PT on Nvidia to a new Wall Street high of $250 from $175. Analyst Ananda Baruah cited the belief that hyperscale and AI factor spending could reach $2T by 2028.

Uber Technologies (NYSE:UBER) +9.25%: Uber Technologies tracked higher last week after the mobility company expanded its partnership with Waymo (GOOG) to parts of Atlanta. 

Losers: 

Palantir Technology (NASDAQ:PLTR) -4.78%

Paychex (NASDAQ:PAYX) -3.47%: Paychex (PAYX) posted mixed fiscal-year 2026 guidance for earnings and revenue growth, as headwinds from its Paycor acquisition and the discontinued Employee Retention Tax Credit program weighed on margins during the last quarter of FY2025. 

Dayforce (NYSE:DAY): -2.53% 

Paycom Software (NYSE:PAYC) -2.12%

Automatic Data Processing (NASDAQ:ADP) -0.92%: Automatic Data Processing entered into a $4.55B 364-day credit agreement and a $2.5B five-year credit pact.  

U.S. Tech-based ETFs to track:  (NASDAQ:QQQ), (NYSEARCA:VGT), (XLK), (NASDAQ:SMH), (NYSEARCA:IYW), (NYSEARCA:FTEC), (NASDAQ:SOXX), (BATS:IGV), (NASDAQ:CIBR), (NYSEARCA:IGM), (NYSEARCA:IXN).

data.title: Parametric insurance is said to gain traction as climate risks grow
data.description:
A once-niche form of insurance is going mainstream, attracting major corporations and investors eager to hedge against climate risks and natural disasters, Bloomberg News reported Sunday.

Parametric insurance, which provides rapid payouts based on measurable events like wind speed or rainfall, historically was used by farmers or small businesses in developing nations. Now, global firms such as Sanofi, Liberty Latin America and Greenbacker Capital are embracing these policies to fill gaps left by traditional insurance, especially as extreme weather intensifies.

The market for parametric insurance is expected to nearly double to $34 billion by 2033, drawing interest from the hedge fund world. Insurance-linked securities (ILS), including catastrophe bonds, have delivered strong returns recently. Investment managers are betting parametric strategies can outperform.

Zurich-based Twelve Capital launched the Lumyna-Twelve Capital Parametric ILS Fund earlier this year, raising €85 million ($99 million) with expectations to hit €200 million next year. The fund aims to return a couple of percentage points above the cat bond market, portfolio manager Rhodri Morris said to Bloomberg News.

A key advantage is speed. Unlike catastrophe bonds, where payouts can take months or years to finalize, parametric triggers provide near-instant clarity on losses.

Still, investors need to be selective. Parametric products are rapidly expanding, from solar farms insuring against tornadoes to hurricane storm-surge coverage for U.S. coastlines. Liberty Latin America secured $44 million in fast payouts after Hurricane Beryl, while Greenbacker Capital leveraged parametric deals to boost financing for its wind projects.

With extreme weather events on the rise, the appetite for fast, transparent risk transfer solutions is only expected to grow.

data.title: India's Torrent to buy controlling stake in JB Chemicals from KKR for $1.4B
data.description:
Torrent Pharmaceuticals reached an agreement to acquire a controlling stake in JB Chemicals & Pharmaceuticals from private equity giant KKR (NYSE:KKR), marking another significant transaction amid India's surge in corporate dealmaking.

According to the terms announced Sunday, Torrent will purchase a 46.4% interest in JB Pharma at 1,600 rupees ($18.70) per share, valuing the deal at roughly $1.4 billion. Following the acquisition, Torrent intends to launch a mandatory open offer for an additional 26% of JB Pharma at a price of 1,639 rupees per share.

The agreement also includes plans for Torrent to acquire up to 2.8% of JB Pharma from certain employees at the same price offered for KKR's stake. Both companies will pursue a merger through a scheme of arrangement, according to the announcement.

Shares of JB Pharma have fallen approximately 2.3% so far this year, leaving the company with a market capitalization near $3.3 billion. The stock closed at 1,801.4 rupees in Mumbai on Friday. Torrent’s shares have remained largely steady this year, giving the company an overall valuation of around $13.2 billion.

KKR originally acquired its majority holding in JB Pharma in 2020 from the Mody family, the company's founders, and subsequently launched an open offer for an additional 26% stake. The exit marks a return exceeding five times KKR’s initial investment, according to a person familiar with the transaction who requested anonymity.

Founded in 1976, JB Pharma develops a range of pharmaceutical products across therapeutic areas such as gastroenterology, hypertension, dermatology and diabetes. The company exports products such as tablets, capsules, creams and other formulations to more than 40 countries, including the United States.

data.title: Biggest weekly communication services gainers: Meta Platforms leads
data.description:
Wall Street’s major averages closed the week higher, as both the S&P 500 (SP500) and Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) notched fresh all-time trading highs and record high closes.

Overall, the sector, represented by the Communication Services Select Sector SPDR ETF (NYSEARCA:XLC), gained 4.8% in the week, while the broader market (S&P 500) added 3.1%.

Weekly top gainers

  • Netflix (NFLX) +7.45%

  • Alphabet (GOOGL) +7.14% and (GOOG) +6.28%

All 23 stocks in the Communication Services sector ended the week with a gain. 

 
 
 

ETFs to tab the communication services sector include: (NYSEARCA:XLC), (VOX), (IYZ), (RSPC), and (XTL).

data.title: Trump's spending bill faces $3.3T deficit warning as Senate battle heats up
data.description:
President Trump’s sweeping domestic policy bill is under fresh scrutiny after the Congressional Budget Office estimated the legislation would add at least $3.3 trillion to federal deficits over the next decade.

The latest projection has amplified concerns among Senate Republicans, several of whom have already voiced unease over the plan’s steep price tag, the New York Times reported Sunday.

The CBO's updated figures reflect the Senate’s version of the bill, which differs in key areas from the package that narrowly cleared the House earlier this month. Despite Republican leaders pushing to fast-track the legislation, the growing deficit estimate has hardened resistance from fiscal conservatives whose support is critical for passage.

The Senate narrowly advanced the bill overnight by overcoming a key procedural hurdle, setting the stage for debate to begin this afternoon. However, final approval before President Trump’s July 4 deadline remains uncertain, with Senate Democrats demanding a full, line-by-line reading that could delay action until at least Monday.

Dear Readers: We recognize that politics often intersect with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: ‘F1: The Movie’ speeds to $55.6M debut, dominates U.S. box office
data.description:
Warner Bros.’ (NASDAQ:WBD) much-anticipated F1: The Movie raced to the top of the North American box office this weekend, delivering a $55.6 million opening, according to studio estimates compiled by Comscore. The racing drama roared ahead of the competition, marking one of the strongest debuts for an original sports film in recent years.

Universal’s (NASDAQ:CMCSA) How to Train Your Dragon held the No. 2 spot with $19.4 million in its third weekend, pushing its domestic total past $200 million. Meanwhile, Disney’s (NYSE:DIS) Elio took third place with $10.7 million, despite a steep 49% decline from its debut frame.

Universal’s (NASDAQ:CMCSA) M3GAN 2.0, the latest installment in the AI horror franchise, launched with $10.2 million, slightly trailing expectations. Sony’s (NYSE:SONY) 28 Years Later, the sequel to the apocalyptic hit, rounded out the top five with $9.7 million, down 68% in its second week.

Disney’s (NYSE:DIS) Lilo & Stitch live-action remake added $6.9 million for a strong $400 million domestic haul after six weeks. Paramount’s (NASDAQ:PARA) Mission: Impossible – The Final Reckoning continues to show staying power with $4.15 million in its sixth week.

Among indie and specialty releases, A24’s Materialists brought in just under $3 million, Lionsgate’s (NYSE:LION) Ballerina earned $2.13 million, and Sony’s (NYSE:SONY) Karate Kid: Legends added $1 million despite a major theater count drop.

Overall, the top 10 films generated $122.8 million in domestic (United States and Canada) ticket sales.

Top 10 Movies — Domestic Box Office (June 27–29, 2025):

  1. F1: The Movie (Warner Bros.) — $55,600,000
  2. How to Train Your Dragon (Universal) — $19,400,000 (Cume: $200,057,000)
  3. Elio (Disney) — $10,700,000 (Cume: $42,225,747)
  4. M3GAN 2.0 (Universal) — $10,200,000
  5. 28 Years Later (Sony) — $9,700,000 (Cume: $50,354,000)
  6. Lilo & Stitch (Disney) — $6,900,000 (Cume: $400,070,904)
  7. Mission: Impossible – The Final Reckoning (Paramount) — $4,150,000 (Cume: $185,996,000)
  8. Materialists (A24) — $2,999,422 (Cume: $30,401,381)
  9. Ballerina (Lionsgate) — $2,130,000 (Cume: $55,473,000)
  10. Karate Kid: Legends (Sony) — $1,000,000 (Cume: $51,592,000)

data.title: Warehouse automation investment set to rise, machine vision in focus: UBS
data.description:
Global spending on warehouse automation is poised to accelerate, driven by technological advances and rising labor costs, according to new survey data from UBS Evidence Lab. The findings highlight strong secular tailwinds for automation equipment providers, with machine vision specialist Cognex (NASDAQ:CGNX) emerging as a key beneficiary.

The sixth wave of UBS's Global Warehouse Automation Business Survey, which polled 130 decision-makers across North America, Europe and Asia-Pacific, found that nearly two-irds of respondents plan to increase automation capital expenditures at warehouses and distribution centers over the next year, with an average budget increase of 5%. Retailers and e-commerce companies are leading near-term spending intentions, but wholesalers and manufacturers appear even more bullish over the next three years.

UBS continues to see structural growth in warehouse automation, with limited market saturation to date, analyst Damian Karas wrote in a June 26 research note, adding that a wave of automation investment will unfold through the end of the decade.

Machine vision demand accelerates

The survey indicates growing momentum for advanced machine vision technologies, critical for automating barcode reading, quality inspection, and item recognition in logistics environments. More than half, or 56%, of respondents expect to boost spending on machine vision tools in the next year, marking an uptick from prior survey waves.

While 62% of respondents have implemented some form of image-based barcode scanning, only 36% of their warehouse space utilizes advanced machine vision technology, signaling significant room for expansion.

Cognex (NASDAQ:CGNX), a leader in the space, ranked as the top vendor under consideration for future deployments, surpassing peers such as Japan’s Keyence and China's Hikvision. Datalogic and Zebra Technologies (NASDAQ:ZBRA) remain the most widely used machine vision and barcode scanning suppliers, with Zebra (NASDAQ:ZBRA) also maintaining strong brand positioning for mobile robotics.

Mobile robots and AMRs gain

Mobile robots, particularly Autonomous Mobile Robots (AMRs), are seeing rapid adoption as companies seek flexible automation solutions that avoid costly fixed infrastructure. An overwhelming 91% of survey participants already use or plan to use mobile robots, reflecting a notable increase from previous survey rounds.

Among AMR providers, Honeywell’s (NASDAQ:HON) Intelligrated business scored in the top tier for customer consideration, with Zebra's (ZBRA) Fetch Robotics positioned mid-pack. European automation players continue to dominate market penetration, although Intelligrated's improving standing suggests growing competitiveness among U.S. vendors.

The rising use of AMRs is a function of their flexibility, Karas said, adding that businesses want automation that doesn’t require being bolted to the floor.

Stock Takeaways: Positive for CGNX, mixed for HON, SYM

UBS views the survey as broadly supportive of warehouse automation equities, particularly Cognex, given its leading position in machine vision supplier consideration and the relatively low ranking of Chinese rival Hikvision.

Zebra (ZBRA) continues to show brand strength across both barcode scanning and mobile robotics, while Symbotic (NASDAQ:SYM) appears to face headwinds with the weakest standing among potential new suppliers. Honeywell's (NASDAQ:HON) performance was mixed: the company's utilization slipped versus past surveys, but future customer intentions for its automation and AMR offerings improved.

While Honeywell (HON) is seeing encouraging signs in warehouse automation, the segment remains a relatively small piece of the broader Honeywell narrative, Karas said.

Honeywell (HON) this year announced a major restructuring plan to split into three independent, publicly traded companies: automation, aerospace and advanced materials. The move, set to be completed by mid-2026 is set to streamline operations and sharpen each business’s strategic focus.

Wave of automation still to come

Despite increased spending intentions, UBS said industry-wide automation adoption remains limited, leaving ample runway for growth. The firm expects rising labor costs, AI-powered optimization and e-commerce logistics demands to continue driving investment through 2030.

The survey also highlighted growing interest in AI-based tools for warehouse layout optimization and digital twin technologies, with Intelligrated scoring favorably among respondents evaluating those advanced solutions.

Overall, UBS maintains a constructive view on the warehouse automation sector, particularly for companies like Cognex (CGNX) and Zebra (ZBRA) that are well-positioned in high-growth subsegments such as machine vision and mobile robotics.

The UBS Evidence Lab survey, conducted between April 17 and May 20, 2025, gathered responses from warehouse automation decision-makers across EMEA (31%), Asia-Pacific (38%) and North America (31%). The report marks the sixth wave of the study on global automation trends.

data.title: Weekly top consumer staples gainers and losers: Estée Lauder Companies lead, Bunge Global ends in red
data.description:
Wall Street ended higher last week, with the S&P 500 and Nasdaq Composite hitting record closing highs amid trade negotiations. Sentiment was buoyed by optimism surrounding U.S. trade progress with China and other partners and the stable Middle East ceasefire, easing broader trade conflict concerns.

The Consumer Staples Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (XLP) for the week, which holds a 6.31% weightage on the S&P 500 ended flat, where the S&P 500's (SP500) ended 3.06% higher. However, the sector's year-to-date performance is considerably higher, +2.47%, which is notably below the benchmark S&P 500's (SP500) 4.96%.

Top gainers this week:

The Estée Lauder Companies (EL) +6.5% after HSBC upgraded the stock to a Buy rating after having it set at Hold. Importantly, the magnitude of the company's restructuring program and recent USD weakness is seen signalling an end to the earnings downgrade cycle.

McCormick & Co. (MKC) +4.5% as it sees itself as well positioned with robust plans to mitigate current tariff related costs, fuel growth investments, and expand operating margins.

Target (TGT) +3.9% as it eyes shipping products straight from its factories.

Brown-Forman (BF.B) ended the week 3.0% higher.

Dollar General (DG) ended the week 2.6% higher as it joins the long list of retailers with special July sales events.

Top losers this week:

Bunge Global (BG) is the top loser for the week, with stock price ended 5.8% lower.

Conagra Brands (CAG) -5.1% as its facing cost pressures for chicken, beef and pork exacerbated by limited pricing power, which faces a “meaningful” headwind to earnings growth along with increased margin pressures, leading BofA Securities to downgrade the packaged food producer to Underperform from Neutral, and shave 26% off.

General Mills (GIS) stock ended 4.7% lower as RBC Capital Markets analyst, Nik Modi acknowledged that investor sentiment around packaged food remains poor and noted that fundamentals have yet to perform.

The Campbell's Co. (CPB) -3.3% on gloomy outlook and the Co.’s own conservative profit outlook for FY25, shares set a new low on Friday after a three-day losing streak.

Archer-Daniels-Midland (ADM) ended the week 3.0% lower.
